ICtoolkit 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Compute mBIC for model objects

Source: R/compute_mbic.R
compute_mbic.Rd

Computes the modified Bayesian Information Criterion (mBIC) of Bogdan, Ghosh, & Doerge (2004). mBIC adds a complexity penalty beyond BIC that accounts for the total number of candidate predictors P and a prior expected model size kappa:

compute_mbic(fit, P = NULL, kappa = 4, ...)

Arguments

fit

A fitted model object of class lm, glm, glmnet, or ncvreg.

P

Integer. Total number of candidate predictors (excluding the intercept). If NULL (the default), inferred from fit.

kappa

Positive numeric scalar controlling the prior expected number of true signals. Must satisfy P > kappa. Default is 4.

...

Currently unused; reserved for future use.

Value

A named numeric scalar (lm/glm) or vector (glmnet/ncvreg) with the following attributes:

fit_class

Character. The primary class of fit.

k

Integer scalar or vector. Number of estimated parameters (including intercept).

P

Integer. The value of P used.

kappa

Numeric. The value of kappa used.

criterion

"mBIC".

Details

$$\mathrm{mBIC} = \mathrm{BIC} + 2\,k_{\text{pred}}\, \log\!\bigl(P/\kappa - 1\bigr)$$

where k_pred is the number of selected predictors (nonzero coefficients, excluding the intercept). When kappa is small relative to P, the penalty encourages sparser models than BIC.

For glmnet and ncvreg, mBIC is computed at each point on the regularization path (one value per lambda).

Inferring P

If P is not supplied, it is inferred identically to compute_ebic():

glmnet

nrow(fit$beta).

ncvreg

nrow(fit$beta) - 1.

lm / glm

length(coef(fit)) - 1.

References

Bogdan, M., Ghosh, J. K., & Doerge, R. W. (2004). Modifying the Schwarz Bayesian information criterion to locate multiple interacting quantitative trait loci. Genetics, 167(2), 989–999.

See also

compute_mbic2(), compute_ebic(), compute_bic(), ic_step()

Examples

set.seed(1)
X <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 20), 100, 20)
y <- X[, 1:3] %*% c(2, -1, 0.5) + rnorm(100)
fit <- lm(y ~ X)

compute_mbic(fit, P = 20)                  # default kappa = 4
#> [1] 434.401
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 4
compute_mbic(fit, P = 20, kappa = 2)       # expect fewer signals
#> [1] 466.8382
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 2
compute_mbic(fit, P = 20, kappa = 10)      # expect more signals
#> [1] 378.9493
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 10
